name: GRAVITY TRAIL - cursor spawns images that fall, bounce, and vanish under physics
ref: Codrops 'Made With GSAP: Building a Fun Gravity-Based Mouse Trail' (May 2026, tympanus.net/codrops/2026/05/20/made-with-gsap-building-a-fun-gravity-based-mouse-trail/); gravity-cascade cursor pattern cited across Awwwards interaction-design collection 2026 (awwwards.com/inspiration_search/interaction-design/)
palette: #0a0a0a (void) + #f0ece4 (bone card backs); images are the only color; impact flash in #e8c547 amber
brief: Moving the cursor across the page spawns thumbnail cards at the cursor position one by one; each spawned card immediately falls under a simulated gravity constant, bounces with damped restitution off the viewport floor, then fades out after one or two bounces. The density of cards on screen at any moment is the visual signature - fast cursor movement creates a cascade while slow movement drops isolated cards. Distinct from draggable-scatter (pointer-throw physics on pre-existing cards; no cursor-spawn cascade) and particle-constellation (static node map). Signature is visible at scroll=0 as soon as the cursor enters. Canvas 2D drives the physics loop; DOM img elements are absolutely positioned and tracked in a JS array with y, vy, vx, elasticity, opacity; no WebGL. Mobile: a tap fires a burst of 3 cards from the tap point. Guardrail: cap at 20 live cards; remove from DOM once opacity <= 0; requestAnimationFrame loop, no GSAP. Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: H-SHIFT - horizontal scroll gallery that graduates to per-image WebGL displacement
ref: Codrops 'Creating a Smooth Horizontal Parallax Gallery: From DOM to WebGL' (February 2026, tympanus.net/codrops/2026/02/19/creating-a-smooth-horizontal-parallax-gallery-from-dom-to-webgl/); Three.js WebGL image gallery scroll tutorials featured in Codrops 2026 Creative Hub (tympanus.net/codrops/hub/)
palette: #f5f0e8 (warm linen track) + #111 (type) + per-image color dominance bleeds into the track edge as a CSS gradient halo
brief: A native horizontal scroll track shows full-height portrait images side by side; as any image nears the viewport center a Three.js WebGL plane replaces the img tag and applies a UV-displacement shader that warps the image toward the cursor like a lens, relaxing back when the cursor leaves. The rest of the gallery stays plain DOM. Distinct from liquid-distortion (full-bleed vertical morph between works; always-on WebGL) and never-ending-parallax (3-layer depth loop; no displacement shader). Signature: the centered image is always under active shader warp at scroll=0. WebGL note: avoid GLSL ES 1.0 dynamic-indexed local arrays since ANGLE returns 0; use a LUT uniform array; preserveDrawingBuffer false (screenshot via html2canvas of the DOM layer); canvas paints before render loop starts. Mobile: WebGL plane disabled; a CSS perspective skewX on scroll velocity replaces the warp effect. Guardrail: only one WebGL canvas active at a time (swap on IntersectionObserver); DPR capped at 1.5. Three.js via importmap; all UI chrome native CSS/JS, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: ASCII REEL - live character-raster gallery, cursor density, click reveals color
ref: ASCII art canvas rasterizer pattern widely referenced on Codrops GLSL tag archive (tympanus.net/codrops/tag/glsl/) and CSS Design Awards generative-art category 2026 (cssdesignawards.com); character-grid portfolio aesthetic cited in Godly 2026 collection (godly.design/sites/)
palette: #0d0d0d (terminal void) + #e8f5e9 (phosphor green characters); click-revealed color is the raw image, no palette restriction
brief: Every work is rendered as a live Canvas 2D ASCII/character raster: the image is drawn to an offscreen canvas at low resolution, each cell's luminance maps to a character from a density string, and the result is painted as text glyphs in phosphor green. Hovering a cell brightens its character and increases local density; clicking flips the entire work from character-grid to full-color image with a scan-line wipe. A monospace font-size slider in the corner adjusts grid resolution live. Distinct from dithered-lo-fi (1-bit Bayer dither via WebGL shader on the image pixels themselves; no characters) and stdout (terminal monospace text layout, not image rasterization). Signature: the first work is already fully rasterized as characters at scroll=0. Pure Canvas 2D; no WebGL. Mobile: pinch to resize the character grid (font-size step). Guardrail: offscreen canvas resampled at max 200x200px per work; rAF drives the render; reduced-motion skips the scan-line wipe and shows instant flip. Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: BROKEN SET - editorial asymmetric grid with per-cell tilt and breathing hover
ref: Envato Elements 'Web design trends 2026: kinetic type, broken grids and the return of visual personality' (elements.envato.com/learn/web-design-trends); broken-grid editorial layout cited in Awwwards 2026 interaction-design SOTD collection (awwwards.com/inspiration_search/interaction-design/) as a reaction to AI-sameness
palette: #f8f4ee (warm paper) + #1a1614 (ink) + #c94a2e (vermilion accent on one oversized cell)
brief: Works are placed on a deliberately broken asymmetric CSS grid: cells span irregular column and row counts, one oversized cell bleeds into the margin, a narrow strip cell holds only a number label, and cells are rotated by small fixed angles (-3deg, +2deg, -1.5deg) so nothing is level. On hover each cell straightens to 0deg, scales up 1.04x, and its drop shadow grows - as if being picked up - while neighbors dim. The breathing-level motion is the personality; no scroll-driven animation required. Distinct from morphing-grid (one set of cells that physically reflows between named formations on scroll) and collage-scrapbook (taped torn-paper clip-path cutouts). The full broken-grid composition is immediately visible at scroll=0. Mobile: grid collapses to a single-column stack; tilts are removed. Guardrail: all animation is CSS transition (transform, box-shadow, opacity); no rAF; no WebGL; no canvas; reduced-motion collapses hover to opacity-only. If any inline SVG label is used, fill/stroke must be hex not CSS var() in presentation attributes. Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: RIPPLE GRID - click origin sends a spatial wave that reveals cards by distance
ref: Webflow ripple-on-click interaction featured in TheeDigital '20 Top Web Design Trends 2026' (theedigital.com/blog/web-design-trends); spatial wave card reveal pattern cited in CSS Design Awards 2026 innovation category (cssdesignawards.com/wotd-award-winners)
palette: #f2ede4 (bone ground) + #1c1814 (ink) + #2c6fad (ripple-blue wavefront); card media provides all image color
brief: The page opens with a grid of blurred, locked work cards. Clicking anywhere on the page sets a click origin point; a Canvas 2D ripple ring expands from that point and every card whose center the ring crosses instantly unlocks (blur lifts, card snaps to full color) timed by its distance from the origin. Multiple clicks fire overlapping ripples, each a different blue-to-white gradient ring. After all cards are unlocked a second click re-locks them outward from the new origin. Distinct from sticky-grid-unfold (scroll drives a spiral unlock sequence from center, no click or ripple) and scroll-velocity-mood (scroll speed changes ambient hue). The locked-card grid with one visible blue ripple ring is the scroll=0 state. Canvas 2D draws the ripple rings; card unlock timing is pure JS (distance / wave-speed = delay). Mobile: tap triggers the same ripple. Guardrail: ripple ring count capped at 8 concurrent; Canvas sized to window and redraws in rAF; no WebGL; reduced-motion skips the wave ring and unlocks all cards instantly on click. Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: DEPTH TILT - per-pixel depth-encoded 2.5D card parallax, no WebGL
ref: 2.5D depth-map tilt technique featured in Awwwards interaction-design SOTD collection 2026 (awwwards.com/inspiration_search/interaction-design/); depth-parallax card hover pattern cited in Figma 'Top Web Design Trends 2026' (figma.com/resource-library/web-design-trends/)
palette: #1c1c20 (deep void) + per-card image palette; depth mask is grayscale, never visible to the visitor
brief: Each work card has a paired grayscale depth map (lighter = closer). On cursor hover, the card's layers are separated in Canvas 2D by reading the depth map: pixels closer to the viewer translate in the opposite direction to the tilt, creating a parallax window effect where near objects slide in front of far objects. The composite is painted back to a canvas element overlaying the img. No WebGL, no three.js: pure Canvas 2D getImageData compositing with a CSS perspective tilt driving the layer-split offsets. Distinct from parallax-depth (multi-layer scene diorama via CSS transforms on DOM layers; no per-pixel depth map) and retro-futurism-holo (conic gradient foil hue shift; no depth separation). The first card is pre-tilted 6deg at scroll=0 showing the depth separation before hover. Mobile: device orientation drives the tilt angle. Guardrail: offscreen canvas at DPR 1; depth map pre-sampled to 64x64 grid and stored as a Float32Array to avoid getImageData on every frame; rAF only while pointer is over the card. Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: ANNOTATED - CSS Anchor Positioning tethered annotations over a minimal index
ref: CSS Anchor Positioning API (Baseline 2026, full support Chrome 125+/Firefox 147+/Safari 26; developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/Guides/Anchor_positioning/Using); Popover API + anchor positioning tutorial on CSS-Tricks 2026 (css-tricks.com/whats-important-3/)
palette: #f9f6f0 (archival paper) + #111 (ink) + #d4a84b (amber annotation callout); one accent per visible annotation, not a spectrum
brief: A pure typographic work index (title, date, medium, one-line caption per row) where hovering any row fires a native CSS anchor-positioned popover that tethers a rich annotation panel (fullsize image, prompt, specs, process note) to the right of the hovered row using anchor-name / position-anchor / @position-try for responsive flip fallback. The entire reveal is declarative: popover=auto HTML attribute + CSS; zero JavaScript for the positioning. A counter shows how many annotations the visitor has opened. Distinct from query (cursor-driven JS floating div that lags with velocity tilt) and manifesto-essay (prose reading column, inline figures). The paradigm is the native browser Popover+Anchor API, replacing a JS tooltip library entirely. First row is in hover state at scroll=0 with its annotation panel visible. Mobile: popover positions below the row (position-area: bottom via @position-try). Guardrail: @supports (anchor-name: --x) gates the enhanced experience; fallback is a plain details/summary disclosure. No inline SVG; no canvas; no WebGL. Native CSS/HTML + Popover API only, NO GSAP or external JS libs.

name: RIBBON RIVER - endless GPU Bezier ribbon winds through the viewport, cards pin to it
ref: Codrops 'Building an Endless Procedural Snake with Three.js and WebGL' (February 2026, tympanus.net/codrops/2026/02/10/building-an-endless-procedural-snake-with-three-js-and-webgl/); procedural-curve portfolio pattern cited in Codrops Creative Hub 2026 (tympanus.net/codrops/hub/)
brief: A single continuously winding organic Bezier ribbon is drawn to a Canvas 2D layer using a recursive subdivision curve built from random control points that are gently animated over time so the ribbon breathes slowly. Work cards are pinned to positions along the ribbon path computed via SVGPathElement.getPointAtLength equivalents on a 2D CatmullRom spline, rotating to match the tangent. Scroll advances a path offset so the ribbon flows upward and new segments generate procedurally, old segments disappear below. Distinct from elastic-ribbon (one static CSS cord the visitor drags and snaps through; card nav, not a living river) and svgmap-journey (a static hand-drawn atlas map with waypoints; no procedural generation). The ribbon with several pinned cards is live at scroll=0, already winding. Canvas 2D only (no WebGL); ribbon path in a rAF loop. Mobile: canvas scales to 50vw width ribbon. Guardrail: path point count capped at 200; old segments dropped once below viewport; no WebGL; reduced-motion freezes ribbon winding and stops path animation (cards still visible). If any pin SVG marker uses fill/stroke it must be hex not CSS var() in a presentation attribute. Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: NOISE PUSH - cursor proximity warps cards via Canvas 2D Perlin noise field
ref: Codrops generative-substrate and noise-field cursor interaction references in Godly 2026 collection (godly.design/sites/); noise-displacement hover pattern cited in Awwwards interaction-design SOTD collection 2026 (awwwards.com/inspiration_search/interaction-design/) and index.dev 'Web Design Trends 2026: AI, 3D, Ambient UI & Performance' (index.dev/blog/web-design-trends)
palette: #fafaf8 (near-white page) + #0e0e0e (ink type) + #ff6b35 (ember displacement indicator ring around cursor)
brief: A clean grid of work cards sits on a near-white page. A Canvas 2D overlay (pointer-events:none) renders an animated Perlin noise field as a faint texture. As the cursor nears any card, the noise field around that card is sampled to displace the card's CSS transform (translateX/Y) by up to 12px, so the card appears to be pushed by an invisible current. The closer the cursor the stronger the displacement; multiple cards can be displaced simultaneously if the cursor is between them. Distinct from proximity-glyph-field (font weight pressure driven by cursor distance in a type grid; no card displacement) and draggable-scatter (explicit pointer-throw; cards must be grabbed). At scroll=0 the noise field is already visible as a subtle texture and the first card is slightly displaced toward the cursor's default center position. Canvas 2D Perlin noise via a pure-JS implementation (no library); card transforms updated via CSS custom properties in rAF. Mobile: device-motion acceleration drives displacement in place of cursor. Guardrail: noise canvas at DPR 1; displacement sampled at 50x50 grid cells, not per-pixel; reduced-motion disables displacement entirely (cards static). Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: LIQUID PAGE - shader-dissolve multi-page transitions, each page a full scene
ref: Codrops 'Building a Scroll-Revealed WebGL Gallery with GSAP, Three.js, Astro and Barba.js' (February 2026, tympanus.net/codrops/2026/02/02/building-a-scroll-revealed-webgl-gallery-with-gsap-three-js-astro-and-barba-js/); multi-page WebGL transition portfolio pattern in Codrops Creative Hub 2026 (tympanus.net/codrops/hub/)
palette: #070708 (void) + scene-specific accent per page (page 1 amber #e8b84b, page 2 cyan #00d4ff, page 3 coral #ff6b6b); transitions are the palette shift
brief: A multi-page SPA (index + 3 work detail pages) where navigating between pages triggers a full-viewport WebGL shader transition (a noise-masked dissolve that sweeps diagonally and reveals the next page's color accent). Each page is a distinct full-scene composition (the index is a minimal type list; each detail is a full-bleed cinematic spread) so the transition itself is the experience of moving between entirely different visual worlds. Distinct from view-transition-morph (single shared-element morphs between index card and detail; same color world; no WebGL) and fold-origami (within-page CSS 3D fold reveal; single page). The index page is the scroll=0 state; one detail page link is pre-hovered to show the transition color accent. WebGL note: avoid GLSL ES 1.0 dynamic-indexed local arrays; use a LUT uniform for noise thresholds; preserveDrawingBuffer:false; canvas paints before render loop. Mobile: WebGL transition replaced by a CSS clip-path diagonal wipe. Guardrail: one Three.js canvas persists across routes, only the geometry/texture swaps; DPR capped at 1; no Barba.js (use native History API with pushState). Three.js via importmap; all routing native JS; NO GSAP or external libs.

name: TYPED IN - works appear as if being typed live, one character at a time, into a grid
ref: Typewriter-reveal interaction pattern cited across Awwwards portfolio nominees 2026 (awwwards.com/websites/nominees/) as a reaction to AI-generated visual sameness; monospace typewriter editorial aesthetic cited in CSS Design Awards creative developer portfolio category (cssdesignawards.com/sites/creative-developer-portfolio/35579)
palette: #f5f2ed (paper) + #111 (ink) + #e03c2c (vermilion blinking caret); image thumbnails appear only after their title is fully typed
brief: The work grid loads as a blank grid of placeholder cells. One by one, each cell's title types itself in character by character at a realistic variable rate with a blinking caret; only when the last character of a title lands does the work thumbnail fade in beneath it. Multiple cells type in staggered parallel once they scroll into view. The typing is the reveal animation: the visitor watches the portfolio materialize as if being authored live. Distinct from boot-sequence (OS BIOS init lines + CRT flash for a full-page boot ceremony; all text; no grid thumbnails) and stdout (a developer control-room CLI aesthetic with a stats strip; not a word-by-word typing reveal). At scroll=0 one cell is mid-type with a blinking caret and its thumbnail not yet visible. Pure JS (setInterval per character, IntersectionObserver to start each cell); no canvas; no WebGL. Mobile: identical, smaller grid columns. Guardrail: pause typing if the tab is hidden (document.hidden); reduced-motion skips typing and shows all titles and thumbnails immediately; no GSAP or external libs; no inline SVG fill/stroke with CSS var() in presentation attributes. Native CSS/JS only.

name: MAGNETIC SNAP - cursor pulls grid cells toward it, snapping back when released
ref: Magnetic cursor / element attraction pattern cited in uxpilot.ai '14 Web Design Trends 2026' (uxpilot.ai/blogs/web-design-trends-2026) as 'micro-interactions: breathing elements that react to cursor proximity'; magnetic-attraction UI interaction cited in Wix '11 Biggest Web Design Trends 2026' (wix.com/blog/web-design-trends) featuring 'oversized spheres and ribbons that move as users move cursor'
palette: #0c0c10 (near-black) + #f0ece4 (bone card face) + #7b68ee (medium-slate attraction aura around cursor)
brief: A regular grid of work cards on a dark field. As the cursor approaches any card, that card is magnetically attracted toward the cursor: translateX/Y is interpolated toward the cursor delta scaled by an inverse-square falloff (strong near, zero at 200px). Releasing the cursor snaps the card back with a spring-damped ease (no library: a simple verlet integrator with stiffness and damping constants). Cards adjacent to the pulled card also shift slightly in sympathy. The cursor shows a slate attraction-aura glow ring that grows as a card is captured. Distinct from noise-displacement-hover (Perlin noise field pushes cards with a field force, no spring snap) and draggable-scatter (cards must be explicitly grabbed and thrown). The grid is fully visible at scroll=0 with the cursor aura live over the first card. Pure JS rAF with CSS custom properties for transforms; no canvas needed (or minimal canvas for the aura). Mobile: touch-start simulates cursor attraction on the touched card. Guardrail: spring integrator capped at 2 cycles per card before settling; reduced-motion removes all translation (cursor aura glow only); no WebGL; no GSAP or external libs. Native CSS/JS only.
